reflection_title: Writer's Block Wasn't The Real Problem
reflection:
*My mind got clearer, my life got calmer, and I had to learn how to write from a different place.*

Tonight I went on a walk with my dog. 

It's funny, because that seems to be the source of my reflections. The truth is that I walk because it helps me just relax and when you relax is when your most creative thoughts show up. Shower thoughts, anybody?

Is winter here, cold as hell but I like to go just with a hoodie, rarely with a jacket, because I want to train myself to think even when the conditions don't allow for proper thinking.

The problem lately is that my mind has been pretty clear for a while. Something my old self would have gone to jail for — figuratively, for the record. My thoughts used to be cloudy, scattered, full of spice. It was awful at the time because when I wanted to focus I couldn't really do it. One thought after another, like a chain reaction. Pew, pew, pew.

Now, I just look at the sky and smile. Look that is cloudy and dark, like the openings of the Harry Potter movies. And I like it. I like to have that problem.

Because for me, in my life, it's no longer a problem.

But for my writer self, it's hell.

### Some people call it The Writer's Block

But is it *Writer's Block*, really? I feel in my case it's something different. I'll make my case in a second.

Let's start with the idea of the writer's block.

From my research, it's a very common topic. In short, writer's block shows up when you are trying to write, and nothing comes up. For hours, for days, for years.

My problem is not that nothings comes up. I have a lot of ideas, I believe. My problem is different; and I will self diagnose because *why not?*

I ran out of meaningful experiences of me drifting. The main goal to start this newsletter was to document my journey, share my struggles, tell my kids, and eventually *you*, all about this mastery path. This only happened because I started writing, because I had to force to self-analyze and apply my own lens to my life. Now, I try to document as I go. I don't want to fake experiences because that's not cool.

The cadence in how drift presents in my life has decreased, precisely because of this journey, as paradoxical as it sounds, I wouldn't be writing about this if I hadn't learn and practice through this lens. In consequence, my mind now remains grounded more frequently, so I observe, and let my day flow with not as many intrusive thoughts that could lead to meaningful content.

Because of *I-don't-know-what*, my writing shifted from documenting to essaying. But I think it was necessary to be where I am at now. The problem is that because of that shift, my writing voice shifted too; and while I liked it at first, because I sounded grounded, I realize now that it sounded monotone. 

And I don't know if I want that. 

I think in part was because at the beginning my first articles with traction used that voice and they got the engagement. I am human too. But it took away some authenticity from me, and with that, the urge to polish every single article I write.

I don't want that.

### Gotta practice what I preach

So during this walk, I had this cheap epiphany.

I own this newsletter. I can change my writing voice...
Thinking too much on how to polish an idea or an article was costing my creativity, and I think in part that is what was just making me look at the sky and not think in other ideas.

So I decided that I don't want to write so polished and structured, but closer to the heart. I want to keep the science, because learning about all of that — psychology, behavioral theory, neuroscience — is what crafted the lens I use to see my life through, and the ideas that helped me develop [*Adaptable Discipline*](www.adaptable-discipline.com) as a way to engineer my practice of discipline.

My writing voice was in the line of the neutral observer. But I started writing from the trenches, and I will go back to that.

You will still see terms like self-governance, agency, coherence, drift. Because I use them in my life, but the reflections you will read from now on will be like this. 

Less essay, more journal page.
### I can't not talk about the science behind this

Writer’s block is real, and there are studies that suggest this doesn’t only happen in writing, but in other areas too where we rely on creativity.

This can happen when we have too many ideas and don’t know how to turn them into something tangible, or when we feel physiologically blocked from accessing new ideas because of stress, trauma, or pressure.

In my case, I think the pressure of having to write something as good is what hurt my writing, and because of that, the spread of my ideas too. So please, do not try this at home. Zero stars. Not recommended.

When you put that kind of pressure on yourself, like I did, the amygdala, which is involved in stress and threat detection, becomes more active. At the same time, the prefrontal cortex, the part that helps you organize thoughts, plan, and think with flexibility, doesn’t work as freely. So it makes sense that instead of ideas flowing, you freeze when trying to think of what to write, especially when a deadline is coming.[^1]

On the other hand, when you take that pressure off your shoulders, either because you decide you don’t give a damn anymore, or because you realize things can be different, your brain has more room to work the way it should, and it becomes easier to think, connect ideas, and write.

That’s why journaling works for so many people. It gives your thoughts somewhere to go. When everything stays in your head, it can feel messy fast. Once you put it on paper, it usually feels a bit lighter, a bit clearer, and easier to work through. There’s also research on expressive writing that points in that direction, even if it doesn’t work the same way for everyone.[^2]

### Did I lack ideas, or was my brain just more relaxed?

I would say it was a mix...

As I said before, this journey has helped me become a better version of myself. I'm not as pulled away from coherence — my own direction — as before. I still make mistakes sometimes, but it's just a regurgitated version of what I have already shared with you.

As a result, my brain now just doesn't ruminate as much; it takes things easily now; it doesn't just act out of ego as much; and I try to remain integral.

So, yeah, I don't *overthink* as much; but at the same time, I don't stress out too much for the lack of ideas either. In a general scenario this is fantastic; it's amazing; and I would give myself pats in the back.

But I want to keep spreading my word. I want to still help others to go through the same journey without going through the same hurdles, and help them realize that discipline is a practice, not a definitive trait. That discipline is just the label for the practice of constant return to coherence. I want to be able to keep showing people that you can engineer your practice, and that Adaptable Discipline was created precisely for that.

So I cannot give myself pats in the back in this field. I need balance.

I think the best analogy I can find for this is *filling a bucket with water*.

Let’s say your faucet is very powerful. The stream of water that comes out of there comes really fast, and is capable of filling buckets with ease. The problem is, when you open that faucet, it takes a lot of strength to shut it back off. So while you try to shut it off, your bucket overflows, spills, and leaves a mess that you later have to clean.

I think this happens a lot with writers that start writing and realize that channeling their thoughts on paper is therapeutic; they end up overdoing it.

Back to the metaphor, let’s say you learn how to use your strength to shut off the faucet. You know you can turn it back on, but even if you learned how to shut it off, it will create a mess again, and it will take forever to fix it. So you just decide, almost unconsciously, not to do it; you just find other ways to fill your buckets. Some might be more cumbersome, but at least they don’t leave a mess behind.

One day you realize you just need to apply some grease to the faucet. Just in the right quantity. You learn that you can control the stream now, and can fill the bucket just enough to do what you want to do with it.

I’m at that stage now.
### It can take a toll on your identity

If you are in this conundrum, your identity might already have been impacted by this. _Maybe this is not for me_, _nobody wants to read my work_, _this is too much effort_ are some of the ways this shows up.

Writing is not the only place you will find this. For other disciplines, the message is the same: _I’m not enough_; the only thing that changes is the packaging.

So please read this, and save it, memorize it or tattoo it on your arm. Just make sure you don't forget it: *Keep going*. And if you already stopped, then focus on *returning*.

*Just return*.

Discipline is the practice of returning over and over to what matters. And if writing is the direction you set for yourself, because it mattered, then why would you let drift to take that away from you? Let discipline be the secret weapon that makes you and your identity indestructible.

One practice that can help you get back to it today is: take that thing that matters to you, and determine what is the minimal action you can take to get back to it. If it's working out again, try a 1-minute plank; if it's writing, start by writing about anything, apply some stream of consciousness, just to get going; if it's going back to a project, check what's the minimum task that will help you move forward.

We all drift; it's human and it's normal. We all have busy lives and priorities.

If this is your priority, treat it like one. Even small actions count. In fact, those are the ones that count the most, because a chain of small actions every day compound more than sporadic, bigger actions across a year.

The consistency is not in pushing through nonstop, but in to keep returning over time.

Once you return, the next step is to learn how can you make it better — my decision of following my own voice — and then just do it.

You will see the change; and it doesn't matter if it's for better or for worse, it's yours, and you got to decide what direction you want to take.

### What I want you to take away from this

I’ll try to keep this short.

What I would love for you to remember after reading this is that writer’s block is sometimes just a fancy way to say that we are not in the space to write; and that can extend for years; and that can apply to other disciplines too. Turns out that we can engineer that, but before doing it, we need to work on our ability to detect drift.

The thing with drift is that you might not have realized that you just stopped doing the things that mattered because life happened, so honing our ability to detect this will allow us to know where and when to practice discipline.

You won’t even notice, but once you start returning you will realize; maybe like me; that noticing these blips will become easier each time, and changing course will become as natural as waking up in the morning.

In our next paid companion we will work on training our detection time; how long do we take to detect drift; so we can take action before we even act out of character.

Until then, remember to take one small action that can help you return.

**Hope you have a wonderful day!**

[^1]: Arnsten, A. F. T. (2009). Stress signalling pathways that impair prefrontal cortex structure and function. _Nature Reviews Neuroscience, 10_(6), 410–422. [https://doi.org/10.1038/nrn2648](https://doi.org/10.1038/nrn2648)
[^2]: Niles, A. N., Byrne Haltom, K. E., Mulvenna, C. M., Lieberman, M. D., & Stanton, A. L. (2014). Effects of expressive writing on psychological and physical health: A meta-analytic review and the moderating role of emotional expressivity. _Journal of Clinical Psychology, 70_(10), 829–844. https://doi.org/10.1002/jclp.22088

related_articles.title: I Don't Want to Write!
related_articles.reason: Directly addresses the struggle with writing and the relationship between creative struggles and discipline.

related_articles.title: My Accountability Struggle: What I Learned When I Couldn't Even Finish This Post
related_articles.reason: Explores the meta-experience of struggling with the writing process itself and honest reflection on setbacks.

related_articles.title: Finding Your Way Back When Purpose Feels Lost
related_articles.reason: Addresses the challenge of reconnecting with purpose when the original motivation or path has shifted, similar to the author's shift in writing voice and clarity.

reflection_social_en.threads:
## Thread 1 — The clarity problem

My mind got clearer and writing got harder. Turns out I'd been writing from the chaos. Now that the storm settled, I had to learn how to write from calm — and I had no idea how.

---

## Thread 2 — The polished voice trap

I started sounding grounded in my writing. Got engagement. Kept doing it. Then realized I'd traded authenticity for a voice that worked. The algorithm liked it. I didn't.

---

## Thread 3 — Running out of drift

I started writing to document the mess. The writing helped me clean it up. Now the mess shows up less often. Paradox: the thing that gave me material also took it away.

---

## Thread 4 — Pressure kills access

Writer's block isn't always about having no ideas. Sometimes it's having ideas but feeling like they need to be as good as the last thing that worked. That pressure blocks everything.

---

## Thread 5 — Less essay more trench

I own this thing. I can write however I want. Took a walk to remember that. Going back to writing from the middle of it instead of after I've figured it out.
reflection_social_en.substack_note:
## Note 1 — The Paradox of Getting Better

The thing nobody tells you about fixing the chaos in your head: you might miss it when it's gone.

Not the suffering. Not the days you couldn't focus. But the raw material. The spice. When your thoughts used to ricochet like pinballs, at least there was always something to write about.

Now my mind stays clear more often. Which was the whole point. Except now I stand there looking at the sky with nothing pressing to say.

The discipline worked. And that created a completely different problem.

👉 [ARTICLE_LINK]

---

## Note 2 — When Engagement Teaches You the Wrong Lesson

I started writing from the trenches. Messy, urgent, real. Then a few polished essays got traction and I thought: oh, that's what works.

So I kept polishing. Kept structuring. Kept sounding like the neutral observer instead of the person actually living it. The engagement said it was working, but the writing started to feel like performance.

You can optimize yourself into a voice that isn't yours. Doesn't matter if people like it.

The price is the urge to write at all.

👉 [ARTICLE_LINK]

---

## Note 3 — The Amygdala Doesn't Care About Your Deadline

Writer's block isn't always about having no ideas. Sometimes it's about having ideas and feeling physically unable to access them.

When you put enough pressure on yourself to write something *good*, your threat-detection system kicks in. The amygdala lights up. The prefrontal cortex, the part that organizes thoughts and plans sentences, starts to shut down.

Your brain treats the blank page like danger. And you can't think your way out of a stress response.

Zero stars. Not recommended.

👉 [ARTICLE_LINK]

---

## Note 4 — Documenting vs. Essaying

I started this to document drift as it happened. To catch myself mid-spiral and name what was going on. The writing was a tool, not a product.

Then I got better. Drifted less. Had fewer urgent things to process. So the writing shifted from documenting to essaying. From "this is happening to me" to "here's what I've learned."

Both are useful. But only one feels like mine.

Less essay, more journal page. That's where I'm going back.

👉 [ARTICLE_LINK]
reflection_social_en.instagram:
## Caption 1 — The Relief That Broke My Pen

My life got calmer and suddenly I had nothing to write about.

That used to be the dream, right? A quiet mind. Thoughts that don't ricochet like pinballs. The ability to just walk the dog without spiraling into seventeen tangents before you hit the corner.

I spent years fighting the noise. Now the noise is gone, and I miss it a little.

Because all my best writing came from the drift. From documenting the mess in real time — the distance between who I was and who I was trying to become. Now that gap is smaller, the urgency is different. I don't have the same raw material.

So I tried to write essays instead. Polished, structured, grounded. They did well. People engaged. But something felt off. I was writing *about* the work instead of *from* the work.

It took a cold walk to realize: I own this thing. I can write from the trenches again. Less essay, more journal page. Less neutral observer, more participant.

The irony is that getting better at life made me worse at documenting it.

What do you lose when you finally get the thing you've been chasing?

👉 Link in bio

#audhd #parenting #executivefunction #neurodiversity #reallife #selfregulation #habitbuilding #relationships #parentinglife #adhdparenting

---

## Caption 2 — When Progress Becomes the Problem

I ran out of meaningful experiences of me drifting.

That's the thing they don't tell you about doing the work. You get better. Your mind clears. The chaos you were documenting starts to quiet down. And suddenly you're staring at a blank page wondering what the hell you're supposed to say now.

My writing used to come from the gap — the space between knowing what to do and actually doing it. That gap is where all the good material lives. The late-night realizations. The moments you catch yourself repeating the exact pattern you swore you'd stop.

Now the gap is smaller. Which is good for my life. Terrible for my writing.

I started polishing instead of documenting. Writing essays that sounded smart and measured. They got traction, so I kept doing it. But I lost something. The voice shifted from "here's what I'm learning in real time" to "here's what I figured out and packaged neatly for you."

That's not what this was supposed to be.

So I'm going back. Less structure, more honesty. Less observer, more participant. Writing from the middle of it, not from the other side.

Have you ever gotten what you wanted and then missed the mess?

👉 Link in bio

#executivefunction #neurodiversity #parenting #audhd #selfregulation #reallife #adhdparenting #relationships #habitbuilding #parentinglife

---

## Caption 3 — The Pressure That Quiets Everything

I was trying so hard to write something good that I stopped writing anything real.

That's what pressure does. It doesn't just make things harder — it makes them impossible. My amygdala lit up like a threat detector every time I opened a blank document. Meanwhile, my prefrontal cortex, the part that's supposed to help me organize thoughts and think flexibly, just... shut down.

Classic writer's block, right? Except it wasn't about having no ideas. It was about having ideas and not trusting any of them. About wanting every piece to land the way the last one did. About forgetting why I started writing in the first place.

I started this to document the drift. To show my kids and anyone paying attention what it looks like to close the gap between who you are and who you're trying to become. Not to perform mastery. Not to write polished essays about lessons already learned.

But somewhere along the way, I started writing for the engagement instead of the process. I sounded grounded, sure. But I also sounded monotone. Like I'd already figured it out.

I haven't figured it out. I'm still in it.

So I'm writing from there again. Less polish, more proximity. Less proof, more practice.

When did you start performing the thing instead of doing the thing?

👉 Link in bio

#adhdparenting #neurodiversity #executivefunction #audhd #parenting #selfregulation #reallife #relationships #habitbuilding #parentinglife
reflection_social_en.linkedin:
## Post 1 — The Clarity Problem

The better your system works, the less you have to say about fixing it.

I spent months building a way to think clearly, parent better, show up more consistently. It worked. My mind settled. The chaos stopped. And then I sat down to write about discipline and executive function — and had nothing.

Not writer's block. Something stranger.

I'd run out of drift. The messy, scattered thoughts that used to derail me? Gone. The executive dysfunction I'd learned to navigate? Managed. I built a system that worked so well it erased the very thing I was documenting.

Turns out you can engineer yourself out of your own material.

If you've ever felt like your progress made you less relatable — to your team, your kids, your audience — this might be why. The gap closes. The struggle becomes competence. And competence is harder to explain than survival.

[insert article URL]

#selfleadership #discipline #executivefunction #performance

---

## Post 2 — Polishing vs. Publishing

Most people think writer's block means nothing comes out.

For me, it was the opposite. I had ideas. I just couldn't let them leave the house unpolished. Every sentence had to land. Every structure had to hold. I edited my way into silence.

The shift happened when early posts got traction. Professional voice. Clean framework. Good engagement. So I leaned into it. Wrote like a neutral observer instead of someone still in the mess.

It worked. And it cost me the thing that made people listen in the first place.

The prefrontal cortex loves structure. It organizes, refines, plans. But when you let it run the show, the amygdala — the part that knows when something's real — shuts down. You sound smart. You stop sounding like yourself.

I own the newsletter. I can change the voice. So can you.

[insert article URL]

#parenting #selfleadership #discipline #habits

---

## Post 3 — When Success Becomes the Enemy

The better you get at managing your brain, the quieter it becomes.

And if your value comes from explaining the chaos — to your team, your kids, your partner — quiet feels like a liability.

I used to walk the dog and come back with five ideas. Scattered thoughts, intrusive loops, the kind of mental noise that people with ADHD know too well. I wrote from the trenches. Documented the drift as it happened.

Then the system worked. My mind cleared. I walked the dog and just... smiled at the sky.

Great for life. Hell for writing.

This is the trade nobody tells you about. You solve the problem you were hired to solve — at work, at home, in your own head — and suddenly you're not the person who understands the problem anymore. You're the person who solved it. Different skill. Different credibility.

The gap between performing well and explaining the struggle? It's real. And it grows every time you get better.

[insert article URL]

#executivefunction #ADHD #performance #selfleadership

---

## Post 4 — The Pressure That Kills the Work

Pressure doesn't just slow you down. It rewires which part of your brain is in charge.

I spent weeks staring at drafts. Not because I didn't know what to say. Because I knew what had worked before — and I was trying to recreate it. Polished structure. Professional tone. The kind of post that gets engagement.

The amygdala, which detects threat, lit up. The prefrontal cortex, which organizes and plans, went quiet. My brain treated writing like a performance review.

This happens at work too. You nail a presentation, ship a clean project, show up perfectly in a meeting. Then you try to do it again — and the system that got you there locks up. Not because you're less capable. Because you're optimizing for the wrong thing.

I started writing to document the mess. The drift. The gap between knowing what to do and actually doing it. Somewhere along the way, I started writing to impress the people who liked the earlier version.

That's not writer's block. That's self-governance breaking under its own weight.

[insert article URL]

#selfleadership #discipline #performance #habits

reflection_es.content:
*Mi mente se aclaró, mi vida se calmó, y tuve que aprender a escribir desde un lugar diferente.*

Esta noche salí a caminar con mi perro.

Es curioso, porque eso parece ser la fuente de mis reflexiones. La verdad es que camino porque me ayuda a relajarme y cuando te relajas es cuando tus pensamientos más creativos aparecen. ¿Pensamientos en la ducha, alguien?

Es invierno aquí, frío como el demonio pero me gusta salir solo con una sudadera, rara vez con una chaqueta, porque quiero entrenarme para pensar incluso cuando las condiciones no permiten pensar adecuadamente.

El problema últimamente es que mi mente ha estado bastante clara por un tiempo. Algo por lo que mi yo anterior habría ido a la cárcel — figurativamente, para que conste. Mis pensamientos solían ser nublados, dispersos, llenos de picante. Era horrible en ese momento porque cuando quería concentrarme realmente no podía hacerlo. Un pensamiento tras otro, como una reacción en cadena. Pew, pew, pew.

Ahora, solo miro al cielo y sonrío. Mira que está nublado y oscuro, como las aperturas de las películas de Harry Potter. Y me gusta. Me gusta tener ese problema.

Porque para mí, en mi vida, ya no es un problema.

Pero para mi yo escritor, es un infierno.

### Algunas personas lo llaman El Bloqueo del Escritor

¿Pero es realmente *Bloqueo del Escritor*? Siento que en mi caso es algo diferente. Expondré mi argumento en un segundo.

Empecemos con la idea del bloqueo del escritor.

Según mi investigación, es un tema muy común. En resumen, el bloqueo del escritor aparece cuando estás tratando de escribir, y no sale nada. Por horas, por días, por años.

Mi problema no es que no salga nada. Tengo muchas ideas, creo. Mi problema es diferente; y me autodiagnosticaré porque *¿por qué no?*

Me quedé sin experiencias significativas de mi deriva. El objetivo principal al iniciar este newsletter era documentar mi camino, compartir mis luchas, contarles a mis hijos, y eventualmente *a ti*, todo sobre este camino de maestría. Esto solo sucedió porque comencé a escribir, porque tuve que forzarme a auto-analizarme y aplicar mi propia perspectiva a mi vida. Ahora, trato de documentar sobre la marcha. No quiero fingir experiencias porque eso no está bien.

La cadencia en cómo se presenta la deriva en mi vida ha disminuido, precisamente por este camino, tan paradójico como suena, no estaría escribiendo sobre esto si no hubiera aprendido y practicado a través de esta perspectiva. En consecuencia, mi mente ahora permanece más anclada con más frecuencia, así que observo, y dejo que mi día fluya sin tantos pensamientos intrusivos que podrían llevar a contenido significativo.

Por *no-sé-qué*, mi escritura cambió de documentar a ensayar. Pero creo que fue necesario para estar donde estoy ahora. El problema es que debido a ese cambio, mi voz de escritura cambió también; y aunque me gustó al principio, porque sonaba anclada, me doy cuenta ahora de que sonaba monótona.

Y no sé si quiero eso.

Creo que en parte fue porque al principio mis primeros artículos con tracción usaron esa voz y obtuvieron el engagement. Yo también soy humano. Pero me quitó algo de autenticidad, y con eso, la urgencia de pulir cada artículo que escribo.

No quiero eso.

### Tengo que practicar lo que predico

Así que durante esta caminata, tuve esta epifanía barata.

Soy dueño de este newsletter. Puedo cambiar mi voz de escritura...
Pensar demasiado en cómo pulir una idea o un artículo estaba costándome mi creatividad, y creo que en parte eso es lo que solo me hacía mirar al cielo y no pensar en otras ideas.

Así que decidí que no quiero escribir tan pulido y estructurado, sino más cerca del corazón. Quiero mantener la ciencia, porque aprender sobre todo eso — psicología, teoría conductual, neurociencia — es lo que elaboró la perspectiva que uso para ver mi vida, y las ideas que me ayudaron a desarrollar [*Adaptable Discipline*](www.adaptable-discipline.com) como una manera de diseñar mi práctica de disciplina.

Mi voz de escritura estaba en la línea del observador neutral. Pero comencé a escribir desde las trincheras, y volveré a eso.

Todavía verás términos como autogobierno, agencia, coherencia, deriva. Porque los uso en mi vida, pero las reflexiones que leerás de ahora en adelante serán como esta.

Menos ensayo, más página de diario.

### No puedo no hablar de la ciencia detrás de esto

El bloqueo del escritor es real, y hay estudios que sugieren que esto no solo sucede en la escritura, sino también en otras áreas donde dependemos de la creatividad.

Esto puede suceder cuando tenemos demasiadas ideas y no sabemos cómo convertirlas en algo tangible, o cuando nos sentimos fisiológicamente bloqueados de acceder a nuevas ideas debido al estrés, trauma o presión.

En mi caso, creo que la presión de tener que escribir algo tan bueno es lo que lastimó mi escritura, y debido a eso, la difusión de mis ideas también. Así que por favor, no intentes esto en casa. Cero estrellas. No recomendado.

Cuando pones ese tipo de presión sobre ti mismo, como yo lo hice, la amígdala, que está involucrada en el estrés y la detección de amenazas, se vuelve más activa. Al mismo tiempo, la corteza prefrontal, la parte que te ayuda a organizar pensamientos, planificar y pensar con flexibilidad, no funciona tan libremente. Así que tiene sentido que en lugar de que las ideas fluyan, te congeles al intentar pensar en qué escribir, especialmente cuando se acerca una fecha límite.[^1]

Por otro lado, cuando quitas esa presión de tus hombros, ya sea porque decides que ya no te importa, o porque te das cuenta de que las cosas pueden ser diferentes, tu cerebro tiene más espacio para trabajar como debería, y se vuelve más fácil pensar, conectar ideas y escribir.

Por eso el journaling funciona para tantas personas. Le da a tus pensamientos un lugar adonde ir. Cuando todo se queda en tu cabeza, puede sentirse desordenado rápido. Una vez que lo pones en papel, generalmente se siente un poco más ligero, un poco más claro, y más fácil de procesar. También hay investigación sobre escritura expresiva que apunta en esa dirección, incluso si no funciona de la misma manera para todos.[^2]

### ¿Me faltaban ideas, o mi cerebro simplemente estaba más relajado?

Diría que fue una mezcla...

Como dije antes, este camino me ha ayudado a convertirme en una mejor versión de mí mismo. No me alejo tanto de la coherencia — mi propia dirección — como antes. Todavía cometo errores a veces, pero es solo una versión regurgitada de lo que ya he compartido contigo.

Como resultado, mi cerebro ahora simplemente no rumia tanto; toma las cosas con facilidad ahora; no actúa tanto por ego; y trato de permanecer íntegro.

Así que, sí, ya no *sobrepienso* tanto; pero al mismo tiempo, tampoco me estreso demasiado por la falta de ideas. En un escenario general esto es fantástico; es asombroso; y me daría palmaditas en la espalda.

Pero quiero seguir difundiendo mi palabra. Quiero seguir ayudando a otros a pasar por el mismo camino sin pasar por los mismos obstáculos, y ayudarles a darse cuenta de que la disciplina es una práctica, no un rasgo definitivo. Que la disciplina es solo la etiqueta para la práctica del retorno constante a la coherencia. Quiero poder seguir mostrándole a la gente que puedes diseñar tu práctica, y que Adaptable Discipline fue creada precisamente para eso.

Así que no puedo darme palmaditas en la espalda en este campo. Necesito equilibrio.

Creo que la mejor analogía que puedo encontrar para esto es *llenar un balde con agua*.

Digamos que tu grifo es muy potente. El chorro de agua que sale de ahí viene muy rápido, y es capaz de llenar baldes con facilidad. El problema es que cuando abres ese grifo, se necesita mucha fuerza para volver a cerrarlo. Así que mientras tratas de cerrarlo, tu balde se desborda, se derrama y deja un desorden que luego tienes que limpiar.

Creo que esto sucede mucho con escritores que comienzan a escribir y se dan cuenta de que canalizar sus pensamientos en papel es terapéutico; terminan exagerando.

De vuelta a la metáfora, digamos que aprendes a usar tu fuerza para cerrar el grifo. Sabes que puedes volver a abrirlo, pero incluso si aprendiste a cerrarlo, creará un desorden otra vez, y tomará una eternidad arreglarlo. Así que simplemente decides, casi inconscientemente, no hacerlo; solo encuentras otras formas de llenar tus baldes. Algunas pueden ser más engorrosas, pero al menos no dejan un desorden atrás.

Un día te das cuenta de que solo necesitas aplicar un poco de grasa al grifo. Justo en la cantidad correcta. Aprendes que ahora puedes controlar el chorro, y puedes llenar el balde justo lo suficiente para hacer lo que quieres hacer con él.

Estoy en esa etapa ahora.

### Puede afectar tu identidad

Si estás en este dilema, tu identidad ya podría haber sido impactada por esto. _Tal vez esto no es para mí_, _nadie quiere leer mi trabajo_, _esto es demasiado esfuerzo_ son algunas de las formas en que esto se presenta.

La escritura no es el único lugar donde encontrarás esto. Para otras disciplinas, el mensaje es el mismo: _No soy suficiente_; lo único que cambia es el empaque.

Así que por favor lee esto, y guárdalo, memorízalo o tatúatelo en tu brazo. Solo asegúrate de no olvidarlo: *Sigue adelante*. Y si ya te detuviste, entonces concéntrate en *retornar*.

*Solo retorna*.

La disciplina es la práctica de retornar una y otra vez a lo que importa. Y si la escritura es la dirección que estableciste para ti mismo, porque importaba, entonces ¿por qué permitirías que la deriva te quite eso? Deja que la disciplina sea el arma secreta que te hace a ti y a tu identidad indestructibles.

Una práctica que puede ayudarte a volver a eso hoy es: toma esa cosa que te importa, y determina cuál es la acción mínima que puedes tomar para volver a ello. Si es hacer ejercicio de nuevo, intenta una plancha de 1 minuto; si es escribir, comienza escribiendo sobre cualquier cosa, aplica algo de flujo de conciencia, solo para empezar; si es volver a un proyecto, revisa cuál es la tarea mínima que te ayudará a avanzar.

Todos derivamos; es humano y es normal. Todos tenemos vidas ocupadas y prioridades.

Si esta es tu prioridad, trátala como una. Incluso las acciones pequeñas cuentan. De hecho, esas son las que más cuentan, porque una cadena de acciones pequeñas cada día se componen más que acciones esporádicas y más grandes a lo largo de un año.

La consistencia no está en empujar sin parar, sino en seguir retornando con el tiempo.

Una vez que retornas, el siguiente paso es aprender cómo puedes hacerlo mejor — mi decisión de seguir mi propia voz — y luego simplemente hazlo.

Verás el cambio; y no importa si es para mejor o para peor, es tuyo, y tienes que decidir qué dirección quieres tomar.

### Lo que quiero que te lleves de esto

Trataré de mantener esto breve.

Lo que me encantaría que recordaras después de leer esto es que el bloqueo del escritor a veces es solo una forma elegante de decir que no estamos en el espacio para escribir; y eso puede extenderse por años; y eso puede aplicarse a otras disciplinas también. Resulta que podemos diseñar eso, pero antes de hacerlo, necesitamos trabajar en nuestra habilidad para detectar la deriva.

Lo que pasa con la deriva es que puede que no te hayas dado cuenta de que simplemente dejaste de hacer las cosas que importaban porque la vida pasó, así que afinar nuestra habilidad para detectar esto nos permitirá saber dónde y cuándo practicar la disciplina.

Ni siquiera lo notarás, pero una vez que comiences a retornar te darás cuenta; tal vez como yo; que notar estos deslices se volverá más fácil cada vez, y cambiar el curso se volverá tan natural como despertar por la mañana.

En nuestro próximo compañero de pago trabajaremos en entrenar nuestro tiempo de detección; cuánto tardamos en detectar la deriva; para que podamos tomar acción antes de que actuemos fuera de carácter.

Hasta entonces, recuerda tomar una pequeña acción que pueda ayudarte a retornar.

**¡Espero que tengas un día maravilloso!**
